channel.title: World News, Today World News, Latest International News, World Breaking News, Trending News of World - Times of India
channel.description: World News Today: Read the latest world news headlines, Current International breaking news world wide. In depth analysis and top news headlines world wide.

image.title: World News, Today World News, Latest International News, World Breaking News, Trending News of World - Times of India

item.title: 400 employees became millionaires overnight when a founder sold his startup for $3.7 billion before its IPO
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Lost beneath Sudan’s sands: Scientists discover 6,000-year-old massive monuments from a forgotten civilisation
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Quote of the day by Banksy: “I mean, they say you die twice. One time when you stop breathing and a second time, a bit later on, when somebody says your name for the last time.”
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Crowdfunded Bid For Spirit: TikTok plan seeks to nationalise airline; over $330m pledged
item.description: Spirit Airlines has ceased operations after decades of making air travel affordable. TikTok founder Hunter Peterson has launched a movement to "nationalise" the airline with collective ownership. This initiative, inspired by the Green Bay Packers model, aims to shift focus from profit to service. Millions have pledged support, showing a growing trend in crowd-equity perception.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: US Firm Revamps Bran Castle: Ad Populum takes 80% stake; aims to boost global tourism
item.description: Bran Castle, the inspiration for Dracula, is entering a new era of international tourism under American ownership. U.S. businessman Joel Weinshanker's Ad Populum, which manages Graceland, has acquired an 80% stake, aiming to boost visitor numbers from the US and Western Europe. This move blends pop culture with historical preservation, ensuring the castle's financial viability and continued allure.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Bad news for Indian Green Card applicants: Final Action Dates for EB-1, EB-2 retrogress in June 2026 visa bulletin
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Scientists revive a 24,000-year-old ‘zombie worm’ from Siberian ice and it starts reproducing
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: King’s College London to merge with Cranfield, create UK’s second-largest university
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: UAE among world’s fastest-growing billionaire hubs with 17 residents worth $212.6 billion: Forbes
item.description: Indian-origin billionaires are increasingly powering the UAE's wealth, with nine individuals on Forbes Middle East's 2026 list commanding $49.9 billion. These entrepreneurs have built multi-billion-dollar businesses across various sectors, reflecting the UAE's appeal as a hub for global wealth due to its business-friendly policies and strategic location.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: The Xi exception: Why we saw a toned-down Trump in China
item.description: During a Beijing visit, President Trump observed China's military might, signaling a shift in global power. While the US faces munitions shortages and slow production, China's defense industry operates at wartime pace, excelling in drones, AI, and satellite tech. This evolving warfare, particularly concerning Taiwan, highlights a contest for future global dominance.
item.dc:creator: Abhishek Mishra

item.title: UK launches world-first roadside ‘laughing gas’ breath test in southern England: New device targets growing drug-driving crisis on roads
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: In 1913, Harry Brearley accidentally created stainless steel while testing rifle barrels
item.description: Sheffield metallurgist Harry Brearley's accidental 1913 discovery of corrosion-resistant steel, while seeking solutions for rifle barrel wear, revolutionized manufacturing. This practical, factory-floor innovation, born from routine testing, proved crucial for industries needing durable, hygienic materials. Stainless steel's enduring relevance highlights how addressing practical problems can lead to globally impactful, long-lasting inventions.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: In 1941, George de Mestral got annoyed by sticky burrs on his dog, and this accidentally launched the multibillion-dollar Velcro industry
item.description: Swiss engineer Georges de Mestral observed burrs attaching to his dog's fur, leading to the invention of Velcro. He discovered the burrs' hook-and-loop mechanism, which inspired a durable, reattachable fastener. NASA's adoption of Velcro for space missions further popularized this biomimicry innovation, showcasing nature's engineering solutions.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Meet Dr Anand Megalingam: From a tractor driver’s son walking 6 kilometres to school to building a NASA-linked space-tech company
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: What is the ‘Thucydides Trap’? Why Xi Jinping brought it up during talks with Donald Trump in Beijing
item.description: Chinese President Xi Jinping invoked the "Thucydides Trap" during talks with US President Donald Trump, highlighting concerns that the rising power's growth could lead to conflict with the established dominant power. This theory, popularized by Graham Allison, suggests structural tensions make war more likely, a concept now central to global geopolitical debates involving trade wars and technological competition.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: UAE clarifies rules for workers changing jobs after contract ends, warns of one-year permit ban
item.description: UAE employees can now legally switch jobs after their contracts end or by mutual agreement, with the Ministry of Human Resources and Emiratisation clarifying new rules. While job mobility has been eased, certain violations like unauthorized absence can still result in a one-year work permit ban.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Why James Dyson kept building prototypes long after most people would quit? This reveals his billionaire invention rule
item.description: James Dyson's journey to invent the bagless vacuum cleaner involved 5,127 prototypes, highlighting the power of iterative design and learning from failure. This extensive process demonstrates that innovation stems from persistent testing, correction, and refinement rather than sudden inspiration. The story emphasizes that embracing failure as valuable data is crucial for product development and success.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Carlos Slim's billionaire crisis rule about 'maintaining austerity in good times' is unexpectedly conservative
item.description: Billionaire Carlos Slim advocates for fiscal prudence during economic booms, a principle that strengthens companies for inevitable downturns. This approach counters the tendency for excessive spending and hidden inefficiencies that high revenues often mask. By focusing on waste prevention and retaining flexibility, businesses build resilience, enabling them to navigate crises without drastic measures.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: The 5 deepest ocean trenches on Earth: Why one basin owns every single slot
item.description: All of Earth's deepest ocean trenches are located in the Pacific Ocean, a result of intense tectonic plate subduction. These trenches, like the Mariana and Tonga, are formed where oceanic plates dive beneath continental plates, creating geological wounds. These dynamic features offer clues to Earth's self-transformation and the potential for life elsewhere.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: America’s first social philanthropist: The secret story of 5,000 schools and a generosity second to none
item.description: During segregation, African Americans faced inadequate education. Businessman Julius Rosenwald partnered with Booker T. Washington and local communities to build over 5,000 schools. This initiative, funded by matching grants and community contributions, provided vital educational spaces. These schools became community hubs, fostering literacy and self-respect. Though many are gone, their legacy highlights community power in overcoming systemic neglect.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk
